front: The one invariant that governs all prompt caching?
back: Exact prefix match. Cache key = rendered request up to a breakpoint, in render order (tools → system → messages). One changed byte invalidates everything after it.

front: Name the classic silent cache invalidators.
back: Timestamp/current-date in the system prompt; non-deterministic serialization (unsorted keys, unordered tool lists); per-user/session IDs early in the prompt; feature-flag-toggled prompt sections; changing tools or model mid-conversation.

front: How do you order request content for cache stability?
back: Most stable first: deterministic tool definitions → frozen system prompt → stable history → volatile per-request content AFTER the last breakpoint.

front: Breakpoint limits, TTLs, and the minimum-size trap?
back: Up to 4 breakpoints; default TTL 5 minutes (1-hour option costs more to write); prefixes under a model-dependent minimum (~1–2K tokens) are silently NOT cached.

front: How do you verify caching works?
back: The usage block reports cache writes and reads separately. Zero reads on repeated identical-prefix traffic = silent invalidator → byte-diff two consecutive rendered requests.
Writes: slight premium over input. Reads: ~an order of magnitude cheaper. Pays for itself by the second hit. Never cache what never repeats — that's pure write premium.

front: When is caching the WRONG lever?
back: Context-window overflow (caching changes cost, not capacity), one-shot workloads (no repeats), and output-generation latency (cached input speeds prompt processing only).

front: Match the cost lever to the expense: repeated prefix / deferrable volume / over-tiered / bloated unique content.
back: Repeated prefix → cache. Deferrable volume → batch. Over-tiered capability → smaller model. Bloated unique content → trim. They compose — cheap model + warm cache is often cheapest of all.
